solution: An antiderivative of \( x^{-[[p]]} \) is \( \dfrac{x^{1-[[p]]}}{1-[[p]]} \). Since \( [[p]]>1 \), this term vanishes as \( x\to\infty \), so the integral converges to \( [[c]]\cdot\dfrac{[[a]]^{1-[[p]]}}{[[p]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps the sign from \( 1/(1-[[p]]) \) without flipping it, the opposite sign of the correct convergent value.
This uses the wrong power (\( [[p]] \) instead of \( [[p]]-1 \)) in the denominator's exponent.
This evaluates the integrand at \( x=[[a]] \) instead of computing the improper integral.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies by \( [[p]] \) instead of leaving the \( [[p]]-1 \) denominator as is.
This divides by an extra factor of \( [[p]] \) beyond the correct \( [[p]]-1 \).

solution: An antiderivative of \( e^{-[[k]]x} \) is \( -\tfrac{1}{[[k]]}e^{-[[k]]x} \); this vanishes as \( x\to\infty \) and equals \( -\tfrac{1}{[[k]]} \) at \( x=0 \). So the integral converges to \( [[c]]\left(0-\left(-\tfrac{1}{[[k]]}\right)\right) = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( [[k]] \) instead of dividing by it, inverting the exponential's antiderivative coefficient.
This forgets the \( 1/[[k]] \) factor entirely, as if \( [[k]]=1 \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is half of the correct value.
This is double the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the improper integral \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[a]]} \dfrac{[[c]]}{\sqrt{x}}\,dx \) (the integrand is unbounded near \( x=0 \)).
solution: An antiderivative of \( x^{-1/2} \) is \( 2x^{1/2} \). Although \( 1/\sqrt{x}\to\infty \) as \( x\to 0^{+} \), the antiderivative itself stays finite there, so \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} \dfrac{[[c]]}{\sqrt{x}}\,dx = 2[[c]]\sqrt{[[a]]} - 0 = [[answer]] \); the improper integral converges.
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of 2 from the power rule on \( x^{1/2} \).
This uses the wrong antiderivative power (\( x \) instead of \( x^{1/2} \)).
This evaluates the integrand at \( x=[[a]] \) instead of computing the integral.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is half of the correct value.
This is double the correct value.

solution: Using \( \displaystyle\int \dfrac{dx}{x^{2}+[[k]]^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{[[k]]}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{x}{[[k]]}\right) \), and since \( \arctan \to \pm\tfrac{\pi}{2} \) as \( x\to\pm\infty \), the integral converges to \( [[c]]\cdot\dfrac{1}{[[k]]}\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}-\left(-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\right) = \dfrac{[[c]]\pi}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( [[k]]^{2} \) in the denominator instead of \( [[k]] \), a wrong power carried over from the original integrand.
This multiplies by \( [[k]] \) instead of dividing by it.
This is double the correct value, as if the arctan range were \( 2\pi \) instead of \( \pi \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is half of the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
